_audit.verdict_notes: The keyed rationale (a single serologic HLA antigen can map to multiple alleles, requiring nucleic acid/PCR methods to resolve) is real HLA science but is NOT established anywhere in the 4 approved sources for HLA. The cited support (SRC-044 lines 9-11, 'DNA Analysis: PCR-RFLP, SSP, Bead-chip') is explicitly scoped to 'red cell genotyping and resolving complex phenotypes' in a red-cell/blood-group-genetics subsection, not to HLA. SRC-044's actual HLA section (lines 69-73) lists HLA 'Testing' as only Lymphocytotoxicity, Flow Cytometry, and Virtual Crossmatching -- no DNA/PCR method is listed there at all, which arguably undercuts rather than supports the premise. SRC-010 and SRC-002/SRC-013 add nothing further on HLA molecular typing. Distractors B, C, D are individually well-refuted by the sources (Class I/II distribution per SRC-010 line 27; validity of lymphocytotoxicity/flow cytometry per SRC-044 lines 69-73; ABO vs. HLA-matching distinction per SRC-010 line 109), and A remains the only medically defensible answer among the four, so I agree with the key -- but the question's central fact is grounded in outside domain knowledge, not in these approved sources, despite being framed and cited as source-grounded.

questions_needing_review.stem: A cord blood bank's initial antigen-level (serologic-equivalent) typing panel reports a banked unit as a match to a recipient at the HLA-A, -B, and -DR loci. Before the unit is released for transplant, confirmatory nucleic acid-based (DNA) HLA typing is performed and shows that the cord blood unit carries a different allele than the recipient within the same serologically defined HLA-B antigen group. How should the transplant service interpret this finding?

options.text: This result indicates a laboratory error, because a single serologically defined HLA specificity always corresponds to exactly one underlying allele.
The nucleic acid-based typing result should be disregarded, since antigen-level (serologic) typing is inherently the more clinically reliable method for confirming HSCT donor-recipient matches.

The unit and recipient share the same HLA-B antigen by serologic-equivalent typing but are not identical at the allele level; nucleic acid-based (DNA) typing provides higher resolution than antigen-level methods and has revealed a mismatch that antigen typing alone could not detect.
Because HLA-B is a Class I antigen restricted to antigen-presenting cells, an allele-level discrepancy at this locus has no bearing on HSCT compatibility.

questions_needing_review.explanation_correct: Antigen-level (serologic-equivalent) typing defines HLA specificities at a broader resolution than nucleic acid-based (DNA) methods. Because a single serologically defined antigen group can encompass more than one genetically distinct allele, a unit reported as 'matched' by antigen-level typing can still be discordant at the allele level. Nucleic acid-based typing is confirmatory precisely because it resolves this level of detail, so the finding reflects a true allele-level mismatch rather than a testing artifact.

option_explanations.C: Correct - reflects the expected higher resolution of nucleic acid-based typing relative to antigen-level typing and correctly identifies the discordance as a genuine allele-level mismatch.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect - this reverses the resolution hierarchy; DNA-based methods are used specifically because they resolve details that antigen-level typing cannot, not the reverse.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect - HLA Class I antigens (HLA-A, -B, -C) are found on nearly all nucleated cells, not restricted to antigen-presenting cells; that distribution describes Class II (DR, DQ, DP).
option_explanations.A: Incorrect - this is the precise scenario nucleic acid-based confirmatory typing is designed to detect; a single serologic specificity can correspond to multiple distinct alleles, so the finding does not necessarily indicate an error.

questions_needing_review.teaching_point: A serologic (antigen-level) HLA match does not guarantee allele-level identity; nucleic acid-based (DNA) typing offers higher resolution and can uncover mismatches within a single serologic specificity.

source_references.supports: DNA analysis is described as used for genotyping and 'resolving complex phenotypes,' framing nucleic acid methods as higher-resolution than basic antigen typing.
MHC Class I (HLA-A,-B,-C) found on nearly all nucleated cells vs. Class II restricted to antigen-presenting cells.
The HLA System section lists MHC Class I/II structure and HLA testing methods (lymphocytotoxicity, flow cytometry, virtual crossmatch), establishing the testing context.
HLA matching requirements noted as a major HSCT concept requiring precise donor-recipient concordance.

_audit.verdict_notes: Same core defect as question 0: the premise that nucleic acid-based typing has higher resolution than antigen-level serologic typing (and can reveal an allele-level mismatch within one serologic HLA-B group) is accurate HLA science but is not stated in any of the 4 sources; the cited SRC-044 lines 9-11 passage is about red cell genotyping, not HLA, and SRC-044's HLA-specific 'Testing' list (lines 69-73) omits nucleic acid methods entirely. SRC-010 line 27 correctly supports rejecting option C (Class I is on nearly all nucleated cells, not restricted to APCs), and general logic correctly eliminates B and D, so A is still the only defensible answer and matches the key. Additionally flagging duplicate-coverage: this question and question 0 test the identical fact pattern (antigen-level match can mask allele-level mismatch; nucleic acid typing has higher resolution) from two clinical framings (unrelated HPC donor selection vs. cord blood unit release), which is substantial conceptual overlap for a 2-question set on one blueprint node.

questions_needing_review.stem: A 68-year-old man with a history of unstable angina is admitted with active upper gastrointestinal bleeding. His hemoglobin is 8.6 g/dL — a level that would not typically trigger transfusion under a restrictive strategy in a hemodynamically stable, non-bleeding critically ill patient without cardiac disease. His physician nonetheless transfuses him. Which of the following best explains the physiologic rationale for extending transfusion beyond the usual restrictive threshold in this patient?

options.text: Administering erythropoietin now will rapidly increase red cell mass enough to restore oxygen delivery without transfusion
Patients with acute coronary syndrome are a recognized exception to restrictive transfusion thresholds because their capacity to tolerate reduced oxygen-carrying capacity is limited

Randomized trial evidence such as TRICC has shown restrictive thresholds to be equally safe across all patient populations, including those with cardiac disease, so the threshold should not be altered
Only the freshest available RBC units should be selected, because storage-related loss of oxygen delivery capacity meaningfully worsens outcomes in this population

questions_needing_review.explanation_correct: The sources explicitly identify acute coronary syndrome as a condition for which restrictive transfusion strategies 'may not yet be supported,' reflecting that the physiologic reserve to tolerate reduced oxygen-carrying capacity is limited when myocardial supply is already compromised, so transfusion is reasonably extended beyond the routine restrictive trigger used in stable, non-cardiac patients.

option_explanations.B: Correct: the sources list acute coronary syndrome (along with severe thrombocytopenia) as a specific, named exception to restrictive-strategy thresholds, consistent with limited tolerance for reduced oxygen-carrying capacity in this setting.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: the sources place erythropoietin/iron optimization of erythropoiesis in the preoperative, elective setting to reduce transfusion risk before surgery — it is not described as an acute rescue mechanism for active hemorrhage.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: this overgeneralizes the TRICC-type evidence base; the sources specifically carve out acute coronary syndrome as a population where restrictive-strategy support is not established, contradicting a claim of uniform safety.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: this is a named board trap — the sources state that large trials have not shown a significant survival difference between fresh and older stored RBCs despite the existence of a storage lesion.

questions_needing_review.teaching_point: Restrictive hemoglobin transfusion thresholds (roughly 7–8 g/dL) are evidence-based (TRICC) for most stable, non-bleeding, non-cardiac patients, but acute coronary syndrome and severe thrombocytopenia are recognized exceptions because tolerance for reduced oxygen-carrying capacity is limited; the EPO-latency and fresh-blood 'storage lesion' arguments are classic incorrect distractors.

source_references.locator: High-Yield Note, Subdomain Node 17 (PBM) – 'Restrictive Threshold Exceptions'
source_references.supports: States ACS and severe thrombocytopenia are exceptions where restrictive-strategy support may not apply, implying a higher effective transfusion trigger.
Places EPO/iron optimization of erythropoiesis in the preoperative/elective context, not as acute hemorrhage rescue.
Confirms TRICC-type evidence supports restrictive strategy safety generally, used here as the baseline being contrasted against the stated ACS exception.
Flags the 'freshest unit' storage-lesion reasoning as a common board trap since fresh vs. older RBC survival was not significantly different in large trials.

Option A is the only defensible answer given the sources; B, C, D are each cleanly contradicted. Two quality concerns: (1) BLUEPRINT-MISMAT

_audit.verdict_notes: All asserted facts check out in SRC-032 (essentials-of-transfusion-medicine-and-blood-management.md): restrictive threshold 7-8 g/dL (line 55), TRICC as evidence basis (line 71), ACS/severe thrombocytopenia as named exceptions to restrictive strategy (line 79), EPO/iron placed under 'Preoperative Anemia' for elective surgery (line 63), and the fresh-vs-stored RBC 'storage lesion' distractor with no significant survival difference in large trials (line 41). Option A is the only defensible answer given the sources; B, C, D are each cleanly contradicted. Two quality concerns: (1) BLUEPRINT-MISMATCH — the assigned node is 'Regulation of Systematic Oxygen Delivery' (S3.b.i, C), which implies oxygen-delivery physiology (DO2/VO2, compensatory mechanisms, 2,3-DPG, P50, oxygen extraction). None of these appear anywhere in the four approved sources; the content actually tested here is drawn entirely from Subdomain Node 17 (Patient Blood Management) and Node 16 (Massive Transfusion Protocols) of SRC-032, i.e., administrative/clinical-practice transfusion-threshold content, not oxygen-delivery regulation physiology. This mismatch is systemic across all 3 candidates, not an isolated tagging slip. (2) Minor AMBIGUOUS-STEM — the patient is actively bleeding, which independently justifies transfusion regardless of ACS status; the stem's framing ('a level that would not typically trigger transfusion... in a non-bleeding patient') partly conflates the bleeding confound with the ACS-exception rationale being tested, though no distractor exploits this so the key is unaffected.

questions_needing_review.stem: A 72-kg woman with symptomatic anemia (hemoglobin 6.4 g/dL, resting tachycardia, dyspnea on minimal exertion) receives 2 units of leukoreduced RBCs to restore oxygen-carrying capacity. A properly timed posttransfusion CBC shows a hemoglobin of 6.9 g/dL, a rise of only 0.5 g/dL. Which of the following is the most appropriate interpretation of this laboratory finding?

options.text: Empiric intravenous calcium should be given immediately, since the blunted rise reflects citrate-induced hypocalcemia from the transfused units
This increment represents the expected physiologic response to 2 units of RBCs, and no further evaluation is needed
The blunted increment most likely reflects a clerical error causing an acute hemolytic transfusion reaction, and the units should immediately be presumed ABO-incompatible
Because the units were leukoreduced, less than 85% of the original red cell content remains, which explains the lower-than-expected increment
This increment is substantially lower than the expected rise of approximately 1 g/dL per unit transfused, so the anticipated ~2 g/dL rise was not achieved and further evaluation is warranted

questions_needing_review.explanation_correct: The sources state that one unit of RBCs typically raises hemoglobin by approximately 1 g/dL in an average adult, so 2 units would be expected to raise hemoglobin by roughly 2 g/dL; a rise of only 0.5 g/dL falls well short of that expectation and should prompt further clinical evaluation rather than being accepted as normal or over-attributed to a single presumed cause.

option_explanations.E: Correct: correctly applies the sourced expected-increment benchmark (~1 g/dL/unit) to recognize this result as blunted and warranting further work-up, without overreaching to a specific unproven cause.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: the sourced expected increment for 2 units is roughly 2 g/dL; a 0.5 g/dL rise does not meet this benchmark, so calling it 'expected' misapplies the stated norm.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect overreach: while clerical error is cited as the leading cause of acute intravascular hemolytic reactions, presuming ABO incompatibility from an isolated blunted increment alone (without other hemolysis findings) goes beyond what the modest increment discrepancy supports.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: the sources tie citrate toxicity and the need for frequent ionized calcium monitoring specifically to massive transfusion protocols with rapid infusion of multiple anticoagulated components, not to routine transfusion of 2 RBC units.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: the sources specify leukoreduced units must retain at least 85% of the original RBC content, so leukoreduction itself is not an expected explanation for a substantially reduced increment.

questions_needing_review.teaching_point: A blunted posttransfusion hemoglobin increment (well below the expected ~1 g/dL per unit) should be recognized as abnormal and prompt further evaluation, but the correct interpretation stops at that recognition — jumping to a specific unproven cause (hemolysis, citrate toxicity, or a leukoreduction artifact) misapplies other sourced facts that do not actually support those specific explanations here.

source_references.supports: States one unit of RBCs typically raises hemoglobin by about 1 g/dL (or hematocrit by about 3%) in an average adult.

questions_needing_review.stem: A 55-year-old man is admitted to the ICU with sepsis but no history of cardiac disease. He is hemodynamically stable, is not actively bleeding, and shows no overt signs of tissue hypoxia. His morning hemoglobin is 6.8 g/dL. Per current evidence-based patient blood management practice, which of the following is the most appropriate transfusion decision?

options.text: Withhold transfusion entirely, since he is asymptomatic and restrictive-strategy thinking prioritizes clinical status over the laboratory value
Order a standard 2-unit transfusion as a blanket order to efficiently bring the hemoglobin above the transfusion threshold
Transfuse to a target hemoglobin of 10 g/dL, consistent with a liberal transfusion strategy, as the traditional standard of care
Transfuse 1 unit of RBCs and reassess hemoglobin and clinical status before deciding whether a second unit is needed
Defer any RBC transfusion decision until viscoelastic testing (TEG/ROTEM) demonstrates a coagulopathy

questions_needing_review.explanation_correct: This patient's hemoglobin (6.8 g/dL) is below the restrictive-strategy threshold (typically 7–8 g/dL), which trial evidence such as TRICC supports as safe in critically ill patients without acute coronary syndrome; current patient blood management practice favors single-unit transfusion with clinical/laboratory reassessment ('why give 2 when 1 will do') over reflexive blanket 2-unit orders or liberal targets.

option_explanations.D: Correct: aligns with the sourced restrictive-strategy trigger being met, the single-unit transfusion campaign for stable, non-bleeding patients, and the practice of reassessing before further transfusion rather than ordering a fixed quantity upfront.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: his hemoglobin is already below the sourced restrictive threshold (7–8 g/dL), so the restrictive strategy calls for transfusion at this point; 'treating the patient, not the value' supplements the threshold with clinical signs, it does not mean withholding transfusion once the threshold trigger is met.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: the sources explicitly describe PBM as reducing use of traditional 2-unit 'blanket orders' in favor of single-unit, reassessment-driven ordering.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: a 10 g/dL target describes the traditional liberal strategy; the sources note trial evidence (TRICC) supports restrictive thresholds as at least as safe in critically ill patients without the noted exceptions (e.g., ACS), which this patient does not have.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: the sources describe TEG/ROTEM as guiding when to transfuse specific coagulation factors rather than using empirical recipes — it addresses coagulation component therapy, not routine RBC transfusion threshold decisions in a non-bleeding patient.

questions_needing_review.teaching_point: For a hemodynamically stable, non-bleeding patient without acute coronary syndrome whose hemoglobin is below the restrictive threshold, evidence-based PBM practice favors single-unit RBC transfusion with reassessment rather than blanket 2-unit orders or a liberal (10 g/dL) target; TEG/ROTEM guides coagulation-factor and component therapy, not routine RBC transfusion decisions.

Describes the 'Why give 2 when 1 will do?' campaign advocating single-unit RBC transfusion in stable, non-bleeding patients.

questions_needing_review.stem: A trauma patient receives 10 units of RBCs that were stored for 39 days under standard blood bank conditions. Pre-transfusion analysis of the units shows a marked decline in the organic phosphate compound that normally occupies the central cavity of deoxygenated hemoglobin and stabilizes its low-oxygen-affinity (tense) conformation. This compound is produced by a branch pathway that diverts an intermediate away from the main glycolytic sequence before the ATP-generating steps are completed, and its production declines as intracellular pH falls during storage. Which pathway synthesizes this compound?

options.text: Hexose monophosphate (pentose phosphate) shunt
Methemoglobin reductase (NADH-diaphorase) pathway
Glutathione synthetase reaction
Rapoport-Luebering shunt
Terminal, ATP-generating arm of the Embden-Meyerhof pathway

questions_needing_review.explanation_correct: The Rapoport-Luebering shunt is a branch off the Embden-Meyerhof (glycolytic) pathway in which 1,3-diphosphoglycerate is diverted by diphosphoglycerate mutase to form 2,3-diphosphoglycerate (2,3-DPG) instead of continuing through the ATP-generating steps of glycolysis; 2,3-DPG later re-enters the main pathway via 2,3-DPG phosphatase. 2,3-DPG binds the central cavity of deoxyhemoglobin, stabilizing the low-affinity T-state and shifting the oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve to the right, which promotes oxygen unloading to tissue. During refrigerated storage, RBC pH falls and Rapoport-Luebering shunt activity declines, depleting 2,3-DPG; synthetic capacity and normal oxygen-release function are restored within roughly 24-72 hours after transfusion as the cell resumes in-vivo metabolic activity.

option_explanations.D: Correct — this shunt is the dedicated synthetic route for 2,3-DPG, diverting an intermediate off the main glycolytic sequence.
option_explanations.A: Distractor — the hexose monophosphate shunt synthesizes NADPH (via G6PD) to maintain reduced glutathione for oxidative protection; it does not produce 2,3-DPG.
option_explanations.B: Distractor — the methemoglobin reductase pathway uses NADH generated by glycolysis to reduce oxidized (ferric) heme iron back to the functional ferrous state; it is unrelated to 2,3-DPG synthesis.
option_explanations.E: Distractor — the ATP-generating arm of the Embden-Meyerhof pathway produces ATP for membrane cation pumps and RBC deformability, not 2,3-DPG, which is instead shunted away from this arm.
option_explanations.C: Distractor — glutathione synthetase participates in synthesizing reduced glutathione for oxidative defense, an unrelated synthetic process that does not affect hemoglobin oxygen affinity.

questions_needing_review.teaching_point: 2,3-DPG is synthesized via the Rapoport-Luebering shunt, a pH-sensitive branch of glycolysis; its storage-related depletion transiently left-shifts the oxygen dissociation curve but resolves within about 24-72 hours post-transfusion as synthesis resumes in vivo.

No approved source directly covers RBC energy-metabolism synthetic pathways for this blueprint node.

questions_needing_review.stem: A 6-year-old boy is started on a sulfonamide antibiotic for a urinary tract infection. Five days later he develops fatigue, scleral icterus, and dark urine. A peripheral blood smear stained with a supravital dye (e.g., crystal violet) shows numerous small, round, refractile inclusions within red cells, and a Wright-stained smear shows cells with smooth semicircular defects along one edge. Laboratory testing confirms markedly decreased activity of the rate-limiting enzyme of a specific glucose-metabolizing shunt. Under normal conditions, synthesis through this shunt directly generates which reduced coenzyme required to keep glutathione in its active, reduced form?

options.text: Reduced glutathione (GSH)
2,3-diphosphoglycerate (2,3-DPG)
NADPH
NADH
ATP

questions_needing_review.explanation_correct: The hexose monophosphate (pentose phosphate) shunt, initiated by glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD), is the RBC's synthetic pathway for generating NADPH. NADPH is the required cofactor for glutathione reductase, the enzyme that regenerates reduced glutathione (GSH) from its oxidized form. Reduced glutathione then detoxifies peroxides and protects hemoglobin from oxidative denaturation. In G6PD deficiency, NADPH synthesis is impaired, so glutathione cannot be kept reduced under oxidative stress (e.g., sulfonamide exposure); hemoglobin denatures and precipitates as Heinz bodies (visualized with supravital staining), which are subsequently pitted by splenic macrophages, producing bite cells on Wright-stained smear and hemolytic anemia. The direct synthetic product of the shunt is NADPH, not glutathione itself.

option_explanations.C: Correct — NADPH is the direct product synthesized by the G6PD-initiated hexose monophosphate shunt.
option_explanations.D: Distractor — NADH is generated by the main Embden-Meyerhof glycolytic pathway and is used by the methemoglobin reductase system, not the glutathione-reduction pathway.
option_explanations.B: Distractor — 2,3-DPG is synthesized by the Rapoport-Luebering shunt and regulates hemoglobin oxygen affinity; it plays no role in oxidative protection.
option_explanations.E: Distractor — ATP is generated by the terminal steps of the Embden-Meyerhof pathway to support membrane cation pumps and deformability, not oxidative defense.
option_explanations.A: Classic board-trap distractor — reduced glutathione is the molecule ultimately protected/regenerated, but it is not the direct synthetic product of the shunt; it is regenerated from its oxidized form by glutathione reductase using the NADPH the shunt produces.

questions_needing_review.teaching_point: The hexose monophosphate shunt's direct synthetic product is NADPH, not reduced glutathione itself; NADPH is required by glutathione reductase to regenerate GSH, which protects RBCs from oxidative damage. G6PD deficiency impairs this synthetic capacity, producing Heinz bodies and bite cells under oxidative stress.

source_references.supports: This source (advanced-immunohematology guide) provides general RBC/enzyme background elsewhere in the document set but contains no content on the hexose monophosphate shunt, G6PD, NADPH, or glutathione synthesis; no approved source directly supports this fact set for the blueprint node.

Zero relevant hits found in any of the four files. The question's own source_references section acknowledges SRC-003 has no content on this topic. Every fact in the stem (G6PD deficiency clinical vignette, Heinz bodies, bite cells), the key, and all option rationales is ungrounded in this specific source set, though independently correct per stan

questions_needing_review.domain: 6. Hazards of Transfusion: Specific Adverse Events
questions_needing_review.stem: A 68-year-old man develops acute dyspnea and hypoxemia with new bilateral pulmonary infiltrates during transfusion of a unit of apheresis plasma. Despite intubation and supportive care, he dies later that day, and the transfusion service determines TRALI to be the most likely cause of death. Which of the following is the required next step in hemovigilance/regulatory reporting for this event?

options.text: No report to the FDA is required, because TRALI reactions are frequently underrecognized and underreported and formal attribution is often difficult
Record the event only within the institution's internal NHSN Biovigilance imputability grading system; external regulatory notification is not required for suspected TRALI deaths
Report the event to the FDA's Center for Biologics Evaluation and Research (CBER) as soon as possible by telephone or electronic means, followed by a written report within 7 days
File a Biological Product Deviation (BPD) report instead of a fatality report, since this pathway applies whenever a distributed product is involved in any adverse event
Submit only a written report to CBER within 30 days, since verbal or electronic notification is optional for noninfectious transfusion complications

questions_needing_review.explanation_correct: The sources identify TRALI as one of the leading causes of transfusion-related fatality and state that suspected transfusion-related deaths must be reported to the FDA (CBER) as soon as possible by oral or electronic means, with a mandatory written follow-up report within 7 days of the event — this reporting obligation applies regardless of how well TRALI is recognized or reported in aggregate hemovigilance data.

option_explanations.A: Incorrect: the sources note that TRALI is underrecognized/underreported as a general hemovigilance phenomenon, but this does not exempt a suspected transfusion-related death from the mandatory FDA reporting requirement.
option_explanations.C: Correct: the sources specify that suspected transfusion-related deaths require prompt oral/electronic notification to CBER followed by a written report within 7 days.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect timeline and incorrect claim that verbal/electronic notification is optional; the source requires the ASAP oral/electronic report in addition to, not instead of, the 7-day written report.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: a BPD report (Form FDA 3486) is triggered by manufacturing-related deviations affecting product safety/purity/potency; it is a distinct pathway from, not a substitute for, the mandatory fatality report for a suspected transfusion-related death.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: internal NHSN imputability grading is a hospital hemovigilance classification tool and does not replace the external FDA fatality reporting requirement.

questions_needing_review.teaching_point: TRALI is among the leading causes of transfusion-related fatality; a suspected transfusion-related death, including one attributed to TRALI, triggers a mandatory FDA (CBER) report as soon as possible plus a written follow-up within 7 days — a distinct requirement from Biological Product Deviation reporting or internal hemovigilance grading alone.

source_references.locator: essentials-of-transfusion-medicine-and-blood-management.md, Subdomain Node 18 – Hemovigilance & Adverse Event Monitoring
source_references.supports: States TRALI, TACO, and hemolytic reactions are the most frequent transfusion-related fatalities reported to the FDA; states TACO/TRALI are often underrecognized/underreported; and states suspected transfusion-related deaths require ASAP oral/electronic report to CBER plus written report within 7 days.

Describes the Biological Product Deviation (BPD) report criteria (Form FDA 3486) as tied to manufacturing/cGMP deviations affecting a distributed product, used to build a plausible-but-wrong distractor distinct from fatality reporting.

review.notes: Facts verified in SRC-032 'Subdomain Node 18 – Hemovigilance & Adverse Event Monitoring': TRALI/TACO/hemolytic reactions are the leading transfusion-related fatalities reported to FDA; TACO/TRALI are often underrecognized/underreported; suspected transfusion-related deaths require ASAP oral/electronic report to CBER plus written report within 7 days. BPD/Form FDA 3486 criteria (manufacturing/cGMP deviation affecting safety/purity/potency) verified in SRC-028, confirming option D is a valid wrong-but-plausible distractor (source never states BPD applies to 'any adverse event'). Key B is the cor

_audit.verdict_notes: Facts verified in SRC-032 'Subdomain Node 18 – Hemovigilance & Adverse Event Monitoring': TRALI/TACO/hemolytic reactions are the leading transfusion-related fatalities reported to FDA; TACO/TRALI are often underrecognized/underreported; suspected transfusion-related deaths require ASAP oral/electronic report to CBER plus written report within 7 days. BPD/Form FDA 3486 criteria (manufacturing/cGMP deviation affecting safety/purity/potency) verified in SRC-028, confirming option D is a valid wrong-but-plausible distractor (source never states BPD applies to 'any adverse event'). Key B is the correct single best answer. However, this question is entirely about hemovigilance/regulatory reporting procedure following a TRALI death - it contains no content whatsoever about TRALI pathophysiology or 'Mechanisms of Lung Damage' (the assigned blueprint node). This is a clear topic/blueprint mismatch; the question would be more appropriately mapped to a hazards-reporting/hemovigilance node than to a TRALI pathophysiology-mechanisms node.

questions_needing_review.domain: 6. Hazards of Transfusion: Specific Adverse Events
questions_needing_review.stem: Blood bank physicians are discussing options for preventing transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease (TA-GVHD) in a severely immunocompromised patient. They note that apheresis platelets already treated with amotosalen (a psoralen) plus UVA light using the INTERCEPT pathogen-reduction system do not need additional gamma irradiation before being issued to this patient. Which mechanism of the amotosalen/UVA process best explains why it can substitute for gamma irradiation in this setting?

options.text: It uses riboflavin (vitamin B2) as a photosensitizer to cause guanine damage to nucleic acids
It exposes the unit to short-wave UVC light alone, without any added chemical photosensitizer, to achieve inactivation
It rapidly cools the unit to a temperature that halts donor lymphocyte metabolic activity
It intercalates into DNA and RNA and forms cross-links upon UVA exposure, inactivating nucleic acids in any residual cellular material within the unit
It passes the unit through a leukocyte-reduction filter that physically removes essentially all donor white blood cells

questions_needing_review.explanation_correct: The approved source describes amotosalen + UVA (INTERCEPT) as intercalating into DNA/RNA and forming cross-links upon UVA exposure, and states this process 'effectively replaces the need for gamma irradiation and CMV testing.' The nucleic acid cross-linking mechanism — not filtration, UVC alone, or riboflavin — is what the source attributes to this specific technology.

option_explanations.D: Correct. The source explicitly attributes DNA/RNA intercalation and UVA-induced cross-linking to the amotosalen (INTERCEPT) system and states this replaces the need for gamma irradiation.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect. Leukocyte-reduction filtration is a separate physical process; the source does not describe amotosalen/UVA pathogen reduction as working through filtration.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Short-wave UVC light without an added chemical describes the Theraflex system, a different pathogen-inactivation technology named separately in the source, not the amotosalen/UVA (INTERCEPT) process.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect. Riboflavin-mediated guanine damage describes the Mirasol system, a different pathogen-inactivation technology named separately in the source, not amotosalen/UVA.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. No approved source describes a temperature- or cooling-based mechanism for any pathogen-inactivation technology; the source attributes the effect to nucleic acid cross-linking, not thermal change.

questions_needing_review.teaching_point: Different pathogen-inactivation platforms (amotosalen/UVA, riboflavin/UV, UVC alone) use distinct mechanisms; know which mechanism belongs to which named system, and that photochemical/UV nucleic-acid inactivation is presented in the source as functionally equivalent to gamma irradiation for this purpose.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 48, Pathogen Inactivation (PI) Technologies, Technology Comparison
source_references.supports: States amotosalen+UVA (INTERCEPT) intercalates into DNA/RNA and cross-links upon UVA exposure, and that this replaces the need for gamma irradiation and CMV testing
Distinguishes Mirasol (riboflavin+UV, guanine damage) and Theraflex (UVC alone, no chemical) as separate PI technologies, used to construct mechanism distractors

questions_needing_review.domain: 6. Hazards of Transfusion: Specific Adverse Events
questions_needing_review.stem: A transfusion service quality auditor reviews the maintenance and QC log for the facility's blood irradiator, which is used to prepare cellular components for patients considered at risk for transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease. The log confirms the annual dose calibration was performed and passed 4 months ago, but there is no record of a dose delivery check having been performed on the day the irradiator was used to treat units that were later issued. According to the standard quality control interval for blood irradiators, how should this finding be classified?

options.text: Acceptable, because a passed annual calibration remains valid for a full 12 months and supersedes any day-of-use verification
Acceptable, because dose delivery verification for irradiators is only required quarterly, similar to centrifuge speed/timer checks
Acceptable, because irradiators only require a weekly saline-fill check, similar to a serologic centrifuge
A nonconformance, because a dose delivery check is required with each day of use, independent of a recently passed annual calibration
A nonconformance, but only because the annual calibration itself is now overdue and must be repeated

questions_needing_review.explanation_correct: The approved source lists blood irradiator QC as 'Yearly calibration (Dose delivery check with each use),' meaning two distinct requirements: an annual calibration AND a dose delivery check performed every day the device is used. A passed annual calibration does not substitute for the missing day-of-use dose check, so the missing record is a nonconformance regardless of the recent calibration.

option_explanations.D: Correct. The source specifies dose delivery checks are required with each use, separate from the yearly calibration; a missing day-of-use check is a nonconformance even with a recently passed calibration.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect. The source lists the annual calibration and the per-use dose delivery check as two separate QC requirements; one does not substitute for the other.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Quarterly frequency applies to centrifuge speed/timer checks in the same QC interval list, not to irradiator dose delivery checks.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. Weekly saline-fill verification applies to serologic centrifuges in the same QC interval list, not to blood irradiators.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect. The calibration was performed 4 months ago, within the yearly interval, so it is not overdue; the deficiency identified is the missing day-of-use dose delivery check.

questions_needing_review.teaching_point: Blood irradiator QC has two distinct components with different frequencies — yearly dose calibration and a dose delivery check with every use — and reliable irradiation is the process step that underlies TA-GVHD risk mitigation for at-risk recipients; missing either component is a nonconformance.

source_references.locator: Section 2, Suggested Quality Control Performance Intervals table
source_references.supports: Lists blood irradiator QC frequency as yearly calibration plus a dose delivery check with each use, and separately lists quarterly centrifuge speed/timer checks and weekly serologic centrifuge saline-fill checks in the same table

review.notes: QC-interval facts are accurately sourced: SRC-027 (domain-2 study guide) Section 2 table lists 'Blood Irradiators | Yearly calibration (Dose delivery check with each use)' alongside 'Centrifuges (Speed/Timer) | Quarterly' and 'Serologic Centrifuge (Saline fill) | Weekly' — supporting that the annual calibration and the per-use dose delivery check are two distinct requirements, and correctly ruling out the quarterly/weekly distractors (C, D) and the 'still within 12 months' distractor (E, since 4 months < 12-month interval so calibration is not overdue). Independent_answer=A matches the key and

_audit.verdict_notes: QC-interval facts are accurately sourced: SRC-027 (domain-2 study guide) Section 2 table lists 'Blood Irradiators | Yearly calibration (Dose delivery check with each use)' alongside 'Centrifuges (Speed/Timer) | Quarterly' and 'Serologic Centrifuge (Saline fill) | Weekly' — supporting that the annual calibration and the per-use dose delivery check are two distinct requirements, and correctly ruling out the quarterly/weekly distractors (C, D) and the 'still within 12 months' distractor (E, since 4 months < 12-month interval so calibration is not overdue). questions_needing_review.domain: 6. Hazards of Transfusion: Specific Adverse Events
questions_needing_review.stem: A hematology-oncology patient considered at risk for transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease requires an urgent platelet transfusion. The only apheresis platelet unit immediately available has already undergone pathogen reduction with amotosalen and UVA light (INTERCEPT); no gamma-irradiated unit is on hand, and obtaining one would delay transfusion. What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Delay the transfusion until the unit can also be gamma-irradiated in the blood bank
Discard the pathogen-reduced unit and requisition a fresh, non-pathogen-reduced unit for separate gamma irradiation before issue
Delay the transfusion until CMV antibody testing on the unit is completed and confirmed negative
Delay the transfusion and instead perform extracorporeal photopheresis on the unit before issue
Issue the amotosalen/UVA pathogen-reduced unit as prepared, without additional gamma irradiation

questions_needing_review.explanation_correct: The approved source states that amotosalen + UVA (INTERCEPT) pathogen reduction 'effectively replaces the need for gamma irradiation and CMV testing.' Because the unit has already undergone this process, additional gamma irradiation or CMV testing is not required, and the unit can be issued as prepared without the delay of further processing.

option_explanations.E: Correct. The source states INTERCEPT pathogen reduction replaces the need for both gamma irradiation and CMV testing, so no further processing is required before issue.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect. Additional gamma irradiation is unnecessary and only delays care, since the source states the pathogen-reduction process already replaces the need for it.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect. Extracorporeal photopheresis is a separate therapeutic apheresis procedure performed on a patient's own buffy coat to treat established graft-versus-host disease or cutaneous T-cell lymphoma; it is not a blood-component processing step and is not described in the source as an alternative to irradiating a donor unit.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect. The source states the pathogen-reduction process also replaces the need for CMV testing, so additional CMV testing before issue is unnecessary.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect. Discarding a unit that already provides equivalent protection per the source is unnecessary, wastes inventory, and delays an urgent transfusion.

questions_needing_review.teaching_point: Pathogen-reduction technology (amotosalen/UVA) is documented as a substitute for both gamma irradiation and CMV testing on a blood component; this is distinct from extracorporeal photopheresis, which is a therapeutic procedure applied to a patient, not a donor-unit processing step.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 48, Pathogen Inactivation (PI) Technologies, Technology Comparison
source_references.supports: States amotosalen+UVA (INTERCEPT) pathogen reduction effectively replaces the need for gamma irradiation and CMV testing, supporting issuing such units without additional irradiation or CMV testing

Describes extracorporeal photopheresis (ECP) as a distinct therapeutic apheresis procedure using 8-methoxypsoralen and UVA on a patient's own buffy coat to treat GVHD and CTCL, distinguishing it from donor-unit pathogen reduction; used to construct distractor C

SRC-023 line 17 defines ECP as a distinct therapeutic-apheresis procedure ('Collection of the buffy coat, treatment with 8-methoxypsoralen and UVA light, and reinfusion... for conditions like GVHD and CTCL') performed on a patient's own cells, correctly supporting exclusion of distractor C. Independent_answer=A matches the key. However: (1) the stem

_audit.verdict_notes: Core facts are sourced: SRC-002 states amotosalen/UVA (INTERCEPT) 'effectively replaces the need for gamma irradiation and CMV testing,' supporting issuing the unit as-is (A) and correctly ruling out B (extra irradiation) and D (extra CMV testing). SRC-023 line 17 defines ECP as a distinct therapeutic-apheresis procedure ('Collection of the buffy coat, treatment with 8-methoxypsoralen and UVA light, and reinfusion... for conditions like GVHD and CTCL') performed on a patient's own cells, correctly supporting exclusion of distractor C. questions_needing_review.domain: 6. Hazards of Transfusion: Specific Adverse Events
questions_needing_review.stem: A maternal-fetal medicine team requests an intrauterine transfusion for a fetus at 30 weeks' gestation with severe anemia. The blood bank technologist selects a unit with the following qualification record: group O, D-negative, collected 4 days earlier, negative for hemoglobin S, CMV-safe, and gamma-irradiated. The maternal antibody screen is negative.

options.text: Collected 4 days earlier (unit age <7 days)
Group O, D-negative
Negative for hemoglobin S
Gamma-irradiated
CMV-safe

questions_needing_review.explanation_correct: Of the listed unit characteristics, only irradiation is specifically tied by the sources to prevention of transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease; the sources state that irradiation is mandatory for intrauterine transfusion, exchange transfusion, and premature neonates <1200 g specifically to prevent TA-GVHD, while the other listed characteristics address separate risks (CMV transmission, sickle hemoglobin, compatibility, and unit freshness).

option_explanations.D: Correct: irradiation is the characteristic explicitly linked to preventing TA-GVHD for intrauterine transfusion units.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: 'CMV-safe' status addresses prevention of cytomegalovirus transmission, a separately listed IUT unit requirement, not TA-GVHD.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: hemoglobin S-negative status is a separately listed IUT unit requirement unrelated to graft-versus-host prevention.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: group O, D-negative selection reflects compatibility with maternal/fetal antigens, a separately listed requirement, not TA-GVHD prevention.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: unit age (<7 days) is a separately listed freshness requirement for IUT units, not tied to TA-GVHD prevention in the sources.

questions_needing_review.teaching_point: An intrauterine transfusion unit must satisfy several simultaneous qualification criteria, each addressing a distinct hazard; only the irradiation requirement is directed at TA-GVHD, and examinees must be able to parse a multi-criteria unit record to identify the correct one.

source_references.supports: Lists IUT unit requirements (group O, Rh-negative, <7 days old, irradiated, CMV-safe, HbS-negative) and separately states irradiation is mandatory for IUT, exchange transfusion, and premature neonates <1200g to prevent TA-GVHD.
States leukocyte reduction (a component of CMV-safe processing) reduces CMV transmission, distinguishing it from irradiation's TA-GVHD prevention role.

However, the stem itself contains no interrogative sentence - it ends with 'The maternal antibody screen is negative' and the options are simply the unit characteristics echoed back with no explicit lea

_audit.verdict_notes: Underlying facts are grounded: SRC-017 lists IUT unit requirements (group O, Rh-negative usually, <7 days old, irradiated, CMV-safe, HbS-negative) at line 47 and separately states 'Irradiation: Mandatory for IUT, exchange transfusion, and premature neonates (<1200g) to prevent TA-GVHD' at line 57 - supporting that irradiation alone among the listed characteristics is tied to TA-GVHD prevention. However, the stem itself contains no interrogative sentence - it ends with 'The maternal antibody screen is negative' and the options are simply the unit characteristics echoed back with no explicit lead-in question (e.g., 'Which characteristic prevents TA-GVHD?'). This is a structural defect: as written, an examinee is not told what is being asked and must infer the intended question purely from the option list's pattern-match to the vignette. While A remains the only defensible answer once the implied question is reconstructed, the missing question stem is a genuine quality problem for exam delivery and should be fixed before use.

questions_needing_review.domain: 6. Hazards of Transfusion: Specific Adverse Events
questions_needing_review.stem: A 950-g neonate delivered at 26 weeks' gestation develops severe hyperbilirubinemia on day 3 of life, and the neonatology team orders a double-volume exchange transfusion. The blood bank has a compatible, leukocyte-reduced, CMV-safe RBC unit less than 7 days old ready for issue.

options.text: Gamma irradiation of the unit
Volume-reducing the unit to decrease additive solution content
Selecting a unit from a male donor only
Washing the unit to remove residual plasma proteins
No additional step is required; the unit as described is ready for release

questions_needing_review.explanation_correct: This neonate meets two independently listed high-risk criteria for TA-GVHD — birth weight under 1200 g and receipt of an exchange transfusion — for which the sources state gamma irradiation is mandatory. Leukoreduction, CMV-safety, and unit age address other hazards (FNHTR/CMV/HLA-alloimmunization risk) but do not substitute for irradiation, so the unit as described is not yet ready for release.

option_explanations.A: Correct: the sources specify irradiation is mandatory for exchange transfusion and for premature neonates <1200g to prevent TA-GVHD; this unit lacks that step.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: washing is not identified in the sources as a step required to prevent TA-GVHD in this population.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: volume/additive-solution reduction is not identified in the sources as addressing TA-GVHD risk.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: male-donor selection is the stated mitigation strategy for TRALI, not for TA-GVHD.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: leukoreduction, CMV-safety, and unit age do not fulfill the separately mandated irradiation requirement for this at-risk neonate.

questions_needing_review.teaching_point: Meeting some product-safety criteria (leukoreduction, CMV-safety, freshness) does not waive the independently mandated irradiation requirement for TA-GVHD-risk neonates; exchange transfusion and birth weight <1200 g are each independently sufficient triggers.

source_references.supports: States irradiation is mandatory for exchange transfusion and for premature neonates <1200g to prevent TA-GVHD.
Lists gamma irradiation of cellular components for at-risk patients including neonates <1200g.
States gamma irradiation is essential for preventing TA-GVHD in high-risk patients (fetuses, neonates, immunocompromised) and separately attributes TRALI mitigation to donor-sex selection.

questions_needing_review.stem: A hospital transfusion service determines that a patient's severe septic transfusion reaction was caused by bacterial contamination of a platelet unit, introduced when the collecting facility failed to follow its written procedure for donor phlebotomy-site preparation. The contaminated unit had already left the collecting facility and been transfused before the lapse was discovered. Which of the following BEST explains why this event must be reported to the FDA as a Biological Product Deviation (BPD), irrespective of whether the collecting facility is FDA-licensed?

options.text: BPD reporting requirements apply only to FDA-licensed collection facilities.
BPD reporting is required only when the deviation results in a patient death.
Because the unit had already been transfused when the lapse was discovered, the event is considered outside the facility's control and is not reportable.
Completing an internal root cause analysis satisfies the reporting obligation, so no external report to the FDA is required.

The event meets all five BPD criteria: it arose during collection (a manufacturing step), deviated from established procedure, could affect the unit's safety, occurred while the facility had control of the unit, and the unit was subsequently distributed.

questions_needing_review.explanation_correct: A Biological Product Deviation must be reported to the FDA by any donor center, blood bank, or transfusion service, regardless of licensure status, whenever an event is associated with manufacturing (including collection), represents a deviation from cGMP or established specifications, may affect the safety, purity, or potency of the product, occurred while the facility had control of the product, and involves a product that was subsequently distributed. This scenario satisfies all five elements: the antisepsis lapse occurred during collection, deviated from the written procedure, could compromise safety, occurred under the facility's control, and the unit was later distributed and transfused.

option_explanations.E: Correct: this option restates all five BPD criteria as they apply to the scenario — manufacturing-associated, a deviation from procedure, a potential safety effect, facility control at the time of the deviation, and subsequent distribution of the unit.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: the source states BPD reporting is required of all donor centers, blood banks, and transfusion services regardless of licensure status; licensure does not create an exemption.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: subsequent distribution of the unit (it leaving the facility's control) is itself one of the five required BPD criteria, not a basis for exemption; the deviation still occurred while the facility had control of the product.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: the five BPD criteria concern manufacturing association, deviation from standards, potential effect on safety/purity/potency, facility control, and distribution; patient death is not listed among them.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: root cause analysis is an internal nonconforming-event management tool; the NCE process lists 'Reporting' (notifying external regulatory or accrediting agencies) as a separate required step, not one satisfied by RCA alone.

questions_needing_review.teaching_point: BPD reporting to FDA is mandatory for any donor center, blood bank, or transfusion service, regardless of licensure, whenever all five defined criteria are met, including that the product was distributed.

questions_needing_review.stem: A hospital transfusion service investigates a severe septic transfusion reaction traced to bacterial contamination of a platelet unit that occurred at the time of donor collection. During the investigation, the quality team identifies several distinct potential contributing factors: inconsistent donor phlebotomy-site antisepsis technique, a temporary staffing shortage, a recent change in antiseptic supplier, and gaps in staff competency verification. The team wants a single tool that categorizes these different types of contributing factors and visually shows how they relate to the nonconformance. Which root cause analysis tool BEST fits this need?

options.text: Risk Priority Number (RPN)
Five Whys
FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)
Biological Product Deviation (BPD) report
Fishbone (Ishikawa) diagram

questions_needing_review.explanation_correct: Root cause analysis includes several distinct tools with different purposes. The Fishbone (Ishikawa) diagram is specifically described as a visual tool that categorizes contributing factors and shows the relationships among multiple different causes of a nonconformance, which matches a scenario with several distinct candidate contributing factors that need to be organized and related to one another.

option_explanations.E: Correct: the Fishbone/Ishikawa diagram is described as categorizing contributing factors and visualizing how they relate to a nonconformance, matching the need to organize several different categories of potential causes.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: the Five Whys is a technique of repeatedly asking 'why' to drill down to one underlying root cause; it is a linear approach, not one designed to visually organize multiple distinct categories of contributing factors at once.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: FMEA is described as a prospective, step-by-step approach for identifying possible failures before they occur, not a retrospective tool for categorizing the causes of a nonconformance that has already happened.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: the Risk Priority Number is a severity/detectability score generated within FMEA to prioritize risks, not a tool for categorizing or visualizing multiple contributing causes.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: BPD reporting is an external regulatory notification process, not an internal analytic tool for organizing or categorizing contributing causes.

questions_needing_review.teaching_point: Select RCA tools by function: Fishbone diagrams categorize and relate multiple contributing factors; Five Whys drills to one root cause; FMEA is prospective; RPN prioritizes risk within FMEA.

source_references.locator: Domain 2 guide, 'Root Cause Analysis (RCA) Tools' section
source_references.supports: Grounds the stated purposes of the Fishbone diagram, Five Whys, FMEA, and RPN as root cause analysis tools. No source content addresses laboratory or serologic confirmation of bacterial contamination itself, so this question substitutes the closest available sourced content (RCA tool selection).

However, this question again fails to test the blueprint node 'Skin Preparation' (S8.f.

questions_needing_review.stem: While a hospitalized patient is receiving a platelet transfusion, the patient develops fever, rigors, and hypotension concerning for a septic transfusion reaction due to suspected bacterial contamination of the unit. The bedside nurse immediately stops the transfusion and notifies the transfusion service, which documents the event. According to the standard nonconforming event management process, which of the following is the MOST appropriate immediate next step for the transfusion service?

options.text: Determine the impact of the suspected contamination on the unit's safety and quarantine any related in-house components from the same donation while the event is investigated.
Report the event to the FDA as a Biological Product Deviation before performing any internal impact determination or investigation.
Release co-components from the same donation for transfusion to other patients, since only the implicated unit itself is affected.
Proceed directly to corrective and preventive action (CAPA) without first determining impact or securing related products.
Defer any action on related in-house components until the root cause analysis is fully complete.

questions_needing_review.explanation_correct: The nonconforming event management process proceeds in a defined sequence: documentation, impact determination, quarantine/recall, investigation, root cause analysis, corrective action (CAPA), reporting, and effectiveness review. After an adverse reaction is documented, the immediately appropriate next step is impact determination (evaluating the effect on the product's safety, purity, or potency) together with quarantine of related products in-house (or recall if already distributed), before proceeding to investigation, root cause analysis, CAPA, and external reporting.

option_explanations.A: Correct: this reflects the impact determination and quarantine/recall steps, which the nonconforming event process places immediately after documentation and before investigation, root cause analysis, CAPA, and reporting.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: corrective action (CAPA) is listed after investigation and root cause analysis in the nonconforming event sequence, not immediately after the reaction is reported.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: quarantine/recall is listed as an early step, before investigation and root cause analysis, so securing related in-house components should not be deferred until root cause analysis is complete.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: this contradicts the quarantine/recall step, which calls for securing affected products in-house rather than releasing related components before impact determination is complete.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect: reporting to external regulatory or accrediting agencies is listed near the end of the nonconforming event sequence, after investigation, root cause analysis, and CAPA, not before internal impact determination.

questions_needing_review.teaching_point: Nonconforming event management follows a defined sequence: document, determine impact, quarantine/recall, investigate, perform root cause analysis, CAPA, report, then review effectiveness.

source_references.locator: Domain 2 guide, 'Management of Nonconforming Events (NCE) and Root Cause Analysis' section
source_references.supports: Grounds the ordered sequence of nonconforming event management steps: documentation, impact determination, quarantine/recall, investigation, root cause analysis, CAPA, reporting, effectiveness review. The sources contain no clinical treatment content (e.g., antibiotics, fluids) for a septic reaction, so this question is limited to the sourced quality-process sequence.

Minor imprecision: option A bundles two sequential steps (Impact Determination, then Quarantine/Recall) into

questions_needing_review.stem: A 32-year-old woman with blood group A undergoes allogeneic HPC transplantation from an HLA-matched unrelated donor who is blood group O (minor ABO incompatibility). She tolerates the marrow infusion without event. On post-transplant day 12, coincident with donor engraftment, she develops a new, unexplained drop in hemoglobin, mild jaundice, and low-grade fever. A direct antiglobulin test (DAT) on a fresh sample shows a mixed-field pattern, and an eluate prepared from her red cells reacts with A-positive reagent red cells. Which of the following best explains these findings?

options.text: A panreactive warm autoantibody, characteristic of autoimmune hemolysis, is present in the eluate
The DAT is a false positive because all antibody-coated red cells were destroyed too rapidly to be detected
A recipient alloantibody directed at a non-ABO antigen (e.g., Kidd or Rh) that was below the pretransplant detection limit is now reappearing anamnestically
A potent autoantibody has transiently suppressed expression of the A antigen on the patient's red cells
Antibody produced by the engrafting donor immune system is coating and destroying the patient's residual native A-positive red cells

questions_needing_review.explanation_correct: In minor ABO incompatibility the donor graft (group O) lacks A antigen, but as donor lymphoid cells engraft they can begin producing antibody (anti-A) directed against the recipient's own red cell antigens. Antibody-coated recipient A-positive cells coexisting with newly forming donor (antigen-negative) cells produce the mixed-field DAT, and the eluate confirms anti-A specificity by reacting with A-positive reagent cells — the expected serologic signature of donor-derived, engraftment-associated delayed hemolysis.

option_explanations.E: Correct: donor-derived antibody against the recipient's native RBC antigen fits the mixed-field DAT and anti-A-specific eluate.
option_explanations.A: A panreactive eluate against all reagent cells regardless of specificity is the hallmark of a broadly reactive warm autoantibody, not the antigen-restricted (A-specific) reactivity described here.
option_explanations.B: A falsely negative (not falsely positive) DAT can occur when incompatible cells are destroyed too rapidly to remain for testing; this trap applies to acute intravascular hemolysis and does not explain a positive, mixed-field DAT.
option_explanations.C: This describes the anamnestic mechanism of a delayed hemolytic transfusion reaction to a recipient alloantibody, but the eluate shows anti-A specificity and this group-A patient cannot produce anti-A herself; the antibody must be donor-derived.
option_explanations.D: Antigen suppression by a potent autoantibody is a described serologic phenomenon causing weakened antigen expression, not active hemolysis with a positive mixed-field DAT and a specific eluate.

questions_needing_review.teaching_point: A mixed-field DAT with an antigen-specific (not panreactive) eluate in an engrafting minor-ABO-mismatched HPC transplant recipient reflects donor-derived antibody attacking residual native recipient red cells, distinguishable from autoimmune (panreactive) and classic recipient-anamnestic (non-ABO specificity) patterns.

source_references.supports: Names major vs. minor ABO-incompatible transplant protocols, anchoring the minor-incompatibility clinical context.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 31 (mixed-field DAT, negative DAT trap), Node 33 (DHTR workup)
source_references.supports: Provides the mixed-field DAT interpretation (antibody attacking a small population of antigen-positive cells) and the DHTR eluate/DAT workup pattern and negative-DAT trap.
Establishes that a panreactive eluate is the hallmark of a warm autoantibody and describes the antigen-suppression phenomenon used as a distractor.

This is real, standard transplant-medicine content but is not grounded in the cited sources, unlike the well-grounded distractors: B (WAIHA panreact

_audit.verdict_notes: The pivotal fact that makes A correct — that engrafting donor lymphoid cells can themselves produce antibody (here anti-A) against the recipient's own residual native red cells in minor-ABO-mismatched HSCT ('passenger lymphocyte syndrome') — is not stated anywhere in any of the 4 approved sources; 'donor lymphocyte,' 'passenger lymphocyte,' and 'engraft' do not appear, and SRC-043's only relevant line names the major/minor topic without mechanism. This is real, standard transplant-medicine content but is not grounded in the cited sources, unlike the well-grounded distractors: B (WAIHA panreactive eluate, SRC-061 Node 14), D (DHTR anamnestic Kidd/Rh mechanism, SRC-058 Node 33), and E (antigen suppression, SRC-061 Node 14). Additionally, SRC-058 Node 31's own description of a mixed-field DAT is specifically 'a recipient's antibody attacking a small population of donor cells' — the opposite direction from this question's donor-antibody-vs-recipient-cells scenario — so the source's illustrative statement doesn't directly support the tested direction, only the general serologic logic. The 'day 12' engraftment timing is not sourced. Despite the grounding gap, A remains independently the best answer by elimination (this group-A patient cannot make autologous anti-A; a panreactive vs. antigen-specific eluate rules out WAIHA; DAT is positive, not falsely negative), so it agrees with the key, but the question's core teaching point is not actually derivable from the cited sources.

questions_needing_review.stem: A 34-year-old woman with AML, blood group A Rh-positive, undergoes myeloablative allogeneic HPC transplantation from an HLA-matched sibling donor who is blood group O Rh-positive (a minor ABO-mismatched transplant). On post-transplant day 12, with early engraftment underway, she develops a falling hemoglobin, rising LDH and indirect bilirubin, and a newly positive direct antiglobulin test (DAT). She has received only leukoreduced, irradiated group A red cells matched to her own pre-transplant type. Which of the following best explains the mechanism of her hemolysis?

options.text: Recipient isohemagglutinins are destroying the ABO-incompatible donor graft cells at the time of infusion
Donor-derived (passenger) B lymphocytes transferred within the graft are producing antibody directed against the recipient's own A antigen
An anamnestic (secondary) recipient immune response to a red cell antigen reintroduced by transfusion
A recipient IgG autoantibody reactive at 37°C is coating the patient's red cells independent of the transplant
Donor T lymphocytes in the graft are mounting a cytotoxic attack against recipient tissue

questions_needing_review.explanation_correct: This is a minor ABO-mismatched HPC transplant (group O donor into a group A recipient). The graft carries immunocompetent donor B lymphocytes that engraft and continue producing isohemagglutinin (anti-A) against the recipient's own persisting A-type red cells, producing DAT-positive hemolysis roughly one to two weeks post-infusion. This is the mechanism captured by the curriculum's 'major/minor ABO incompatibility in HSCT' concept.

option_explanations.B: Correct: donor passenger lymphocytes producing antibody against the recipient's RBC antigen is the mechanism underlying hemolysis in a minor ABO-mismatched transplant.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: this describes warm autoimmune hemolytic anemia, an IgG-autoantibody process defined in the sources as a distinct entity from the transplant-associated antibody mechanism at play here.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: cytotoxic donor T-lymphocyte attack on recipient tissue describes TA-GVHD (prevented by irradiation of cellular components), a T-cell-mediated process, not the antibody-mediated hemolysis seen here.
option_explanations.C: Incorrect: a newly positive DAT/antibody screen from a secondary recipient immune response describes a delayed hemolytic transfusion reaction, which reflects the recipient's own memory response, not donor-derived antibody.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: this describes major, not minor, ABO incompatibility — the opposite antibody direction — and would threaten the infused graft cells at the time of infusion, not cause delayed hemolysis of the recipient's own red cells.

questions_needing_review.teaching_point: In minor ABO-mismatched HPC transplantation, hemolysis arises from donor passenger lymphocytes producing antibody against the recipient's own red cell antigens — mechanistically distinct from autoimmune hemolysis, TA-GVHD, delayed hemolytic transfusion reaction, or major ABO mismatch.

source_references.supports: Lists 'major/minor ABO incompatibility in HSCT' as a major concept within the HPC Transplantation domain, the framework underlying donor-antibody vs recipient-antigen mismatch direction.

SRC-002 and SRC-001 contain no HSCT/transplant content at all. The distractors (Warm AIHA/IgG, TA-GVHD/irradiation, delayed HTR/anamnestic response) are each individually well-sourced (S

The only on-topic source text in the whole corpus is a bare five-word phrase in SRC-010 line 109: 'major/minor ABO incompatibility in HSCT,' with zero elaboration on mechanism, direction, timing, or antibody origin. Independently, A is still the best answer on general clinical grounds, and no other option is defensible, so answer_agrees is true despite the sourcing failure.

questions_needing_review.stem: A group A Rh-positive patient received a minor ABO-mismatched allogeneic HPC transplant from a group O Rh-positive donor. On post-transplant day 12 she has a positive DAT, a falling hemoglobin, and an eluate confirming donor-derived anti-A coating her red cells. She remains transfusion-dependent and has no additional red cell alloantibodies. Which of the following is the most appropriate red cell selection strategy for this patient at this time?

options.text: Withhold all red cell transfusion until hemoglobin falls below a fixed numerical threshold, regardless of symptoms
Gamma-irradiate all red cell units to stop further passenger-lymphocyte antibody production
Transfuse group O red cells until the donor-derived antibody clears
Continue transfusing group A red cells, since that was her pre-transplant type
Transfuse group AB red cells for broader antigen coverage

questions_needing_review.explanation_correct: The hemolytic antibody is donor-derived anti-A directed at the recipient's own A antigen. Red cells lacking the A antigen (group O) avoid the antibody's target, consistent with the principle of selecting group O, compatible units when an antibody complicates typing/compatibility. Transfusion should still be guided by the patient's clinical need for oxygen-carrying capacity rather than a fixed hemoglobin number.

option_explanations.C: Correct: group O red cells lack the A antigen targeted by the donor-derived antibody, consistent with selecting compatible group O units when antibody issues complicate transfusion.
option_explanations.D: Incorrect: the patient's native/pre-transplant group A red cells carry the A antigen and would be hemolyzed by the anti-A antibody.
option_explanations.E: Incorrect: group AB red cells also express the A antigen and would likewise be targeted by the antibody.
option_explanations.A: Incorrect: transfusion decisions should be guided by the patient's clinical need for oxygen-carrying capacity rather than simply reaching a fixed hemoglobin number.
option_explanations.B: Incorrect mechanism: gamma irradiation of components inactivates donor T lymphocytes to prevent TA-GVHD; it does not address antibody production by already-engrafted donor B lymphocytes or treat ongoing hemolysis.

questions_needing_review.teaching_point: When a donor-derived antibody is hemolyzing recipient red cells after a minor ABO-mismatched transplant, select red cell units lacking the targeted antigen (e.g., group O when the antibody is anti-A), and base ongoing transfusion on clinical need rather than a fixed numeric threshold; irradiation addresses TA-GVHD risk, not antibody-mediated hemolysis.

source_references.supports: States that when identification is incomplete, Group O, Rh-compatible units are issued with physician approval — adapted here to select antigen-negative (group O) units in an antibody-complicated setting.
States transfusion decisions should be based on the patient's need for increased oxygen-carrying capacity rather than merely reaching a numerical hemoglobin target.

source_references.locator: Domain V, Hazards of Transfusion, High-Yield Facts
source_references.supports: States TA-GVHD is prevented by gamma irradiation of donor T lymphocytes, used to construct the irradiation-mechanism distractor.

source_references.locator: Domain VIII, HPC Transplantation, Major Concepts
source_references.supports: Lists 'major/minor ABO incompatibility in HSCT' as a major concept, supporting the O-into-A mismatch framework of the stem.
